meaning_en: Something that appears bad but results in good
example: His illness, though painful, was a blessing in disguise as it led him to slow down.
Freelance writers are a dime a dozen; clients look for proven track records.
Being the only engineer in a room full of lawyers, he felt like a fish out of water.

meaning_en: Great fuss over a trivial matter
example: The so-called office crisis was nothing but a storm in a teacup — resolved in hours.
Following that rumour turned out to be a complete wild goose chase.

meaning_en: To worsen an already bad situation
example: His unnecessary remark only added fuel to the fire during the heated discussion.

meaning_en: In great hurry to meet deadline
example: The team worked frantically against the clock to submit the proposal before midnight.
When the system crashed, the new intern was completely all at sea.
Go ahead and present your plan — the committee is all ears.
We are all facing the same problem, so we are all in the same boat.
All of a sudden, the lights went out and the room fell dark.
The ancestral property became an apple of discord among the four siblings.
A justice system based purely on an eye for an eye eventually blinds everyone.
Despite the aggressive questioning, she remained as cool as a cucumber throughout.
Even at seventy, grandfather is as fit as a fiddle and walks five kilometres daily.
The toddler sat quietly and was as good as gold throughout the ceremony.
After two days of rest, she felt as right as rain again.
The judge ensured he kept all parties at arm's length to preserve impartiality.
The two neighbouring states have been at daggers drawn over the river water dispute.
She was at her wit's end after three sleepless nights with the project.
After the manager resigned without notice, the department was at sixes and sevens.
The deal was finalised at the eleventh hour before the deadline.

meaning_en: To start again from the beginning
example: After the merger fell through, the company was back to square one.
If you think I took the files, you are completely barking up the wrong tree.
Stop beating around the bush and simply tell me what the problem is.

meaning_en: To do something before someone else
example: Our rival beat us to the punch by launching their product two weeks earlier.

meaning_en: To try very hard to help
example: The embassy staff bent over backwards to help the stranded travellers.
She was beside herself with worry when her child did not return home on time.

word: Between the devil and the deep sea
example: Either accept the unfair terms or lose the contract — truly between the devil and the deep sea.
His dedication to the cause is beyond doubt and recognised by everyone.

word: Bite off more than one can chew
example: Taking three projects simultaneously meant he had bitten off more than he could chew.

meaning_en: To face a painful situation bravely
example: She bit the bullet and submitted her resignation despite the uncertainty.
She blows her own trumpet at every opportunity, making colleagues uncomfortable.
The prince was clearly blue blood, raised with every privilege imaginable.
He told a light joke to break the ice at the start of the tense meeting.
The journalist's investigation brought several corruption scandals to light.
She burned the midnight oil for weeks to prepare for the civil service exam.
By and large, the conference was well-organised and very informative.
Her skills in public speaking have improved by leaps and bounds this year.

word: By the skin of one's teeth
example: He passed the qualifying round by the skin of his teeth.
Unlike most politicians, she always calls a spade a spade without hesitation.
With a brilliant closing argument, the defence carried the day in court.
The two siblings are clearly cast in the same mould — both stubborn perfectionists.

meaning_hi: अयोग्य व्यक्ति को मूल्यवान वस्तु देना
meaning_en: To offer something valuable to someone who cannot appreciate it
example: Sharing advanced research with an indifferent audience is casting pearls before swine.

meaning_en: To catch someone in the act of wrongdoing
example: The accountant was caught red-handed taking cash from the company safe.
With nothing to lose, she chanced her arm and applied for the dream job.
He has had a chip on his shoulder ever since he was passed over for promotion.
New evidence came to light that completely changed the direction of the trial.
It took him months to come to terms with the sudden loss of his father.
She promised to stand by her principles, come what may.
Knowing he was in trouble, he quickly cooked up a story for his parents.

word: Cost an arm and a leg
example: Fixing the car after the accident cost an arm and a leg.
He shed crocodile tears at the funeral of the man he had betrayed.
It never even crossed my mind that he would resign so suddenly.

meaning_en: To regret what cannot be undone
example: The damage is done; there is no use crying over spilt milk now.
Without preparation, he cut a sorry figure during the group discussion round.

meaning_en: To do something cheaply or badly
example: The builder cut corners on materials, which is why the roof collapsed.

meaning_en: To solve a difficult problem boldly
example: The new CEO cut the Gordian knot by simply dissolving the problematic division.
Her dismissive comment about his effort cut him to the quick.
He spent years dancing attendance on the minister, hoping for a favour.

meaning_en: A person whose abilities are hidden
example: No one expected it, but the young debutant turned out to be the dark horse of the tournament.

meaning_en: A law or rule no longer observed
example: The law against littering has become a dead letter in this city.
By the time the guests left, the children were dead to the world.

meaning_en: One who argues against a position
example: She played devil's advocate to stress-test every assumption in the strategy.
He is a die-hard supporter of the team who never misses a single match.
It is time to do away with the outdated rules that hinder progress.
He did his level best, but the competition was simply too tough this year.

meaning_hi: स्वयं उपयोग न करना और दूसरों को भी न करने देना
meaning_en: One who prevents others from using something
example: He refused to let anyone use the hall even when he had no need for it — a real dog in the manger.
I haven't visited my hometown for donkey's years and miss it dearly.

word: Dot the i's and cross the t's
example: Before signing the contract, make sure to dot the i's and cross the t's.
He has been down in the dumps since the business deal fell through.

meaning_en: To fail to recall or find
example: When asked for the capital of the country, he completely drew a blank.
The manager draws the line at personal insults during team disagreements.
His constant humming during work drives his colleagues up the wall.
The government's relief fund is just a drop in the ocean compared to the actual need.

meaning_en: To keep thinking about past events
example: She tends to dwell on the past instead of focusing on future opportunities.
The proofreader's eagle eye caught every single typographical error in the report.

meaning_en: A person who wakes or arrives early
example: She is an early bird who arrives at the office an hour before everyone else.

meaning_en: To admit one's error and face humiliation
example: After boasting about his plan, he had to eat humble pie when it failed.

meaning_en: To admit that something said was wrong
example: She laughed at his business idea, but she had to eat her words when it succeeded.
The climax of the thriller kept the entire audience on the edge of their seats.

meaning_hi: स्पष्ट समस्या जिसे अनदेखा किया जाए
meaning_en: An obvious issue ignored by everyone
example: No one mentioned the budget deficit, but it was the elephant in the room.

meaning_en: People with little knowledge talk the most
example: The loudest critic in the room contributed nothing — empty vessels make much noise.
All their grand promises and plans ended in smoke after the election.
After three rounds of funding failed, they knew it was the end of the road for the startup.

meaning_en: Freedom to cause one's own failure
example: The chairman gave the new director enough rope to hang himself and he did.
With both teams equally matched, it is even odds who will win tonight.

word: Every cloud has a silver lining
meaning_en: Hope or good aspect in every bad situation
example: Losing the job was hard, but it led him to a better one — every cloud has a silver lining.
The defence lawyer used every trick in the book to delay the verdict.
The investigators explored every avenue before concluding there was no foul play.
A great architect needs a sharp eye for detail at every stage of design.

meaning_en: To desire more than one can handle
example: He ordered five dishes and couldn't finish two — eyes clearly bigger than his stomach.

meaning_en: To accept the consequences of one's actions
example: You made the mistake; now you must face the music and accept the consequences.
The award was given fairly — she won it fair and square with her hard work.

meaning_en: A friend who supports only in good times
example: When trouble came, his so-called friends vanished — they were all fair weather friends.
The comedian's opening joke fell flat and the audience sat in silence.
Volunteers came from far and wide to help with the flood relief work.

meaning_en: An achievement to be proud of
example: Cracking the UPSC exam at the first attempt is a real feather in her cap.
Good mentors who genuinely invest in others are few and far between.
He fights shy of any confrontation and always avoids difficult conversations.
After months of searching, they finally found a candidate who fills the bill.

meaning_en: To become confident in a situation
example: It took her almost a year to find her feet in the new city and new role.
The opportunist fished in troubled waters during the political crisis and gained influence.
He is only flesh and blood — he cannot be expected to bear everything alone.

meaning_en: A small defect that spoils something
example: Everything was arranged perfectly; the venue cancellation was the fly in the ointment.

meaning_en: To do the same as others
example: When the market leader reduced prices, all competitors quickly followed suit.
Her speech on climate action gave us all serious food for thought.
The government agreed to foot the bill for the rehabilitation of flood victims.
After twenty years abroad, she finally came home for good.
He was sent from pillar to post by various officials before getting his documents approved.
The opposition party is steadily gaining ground in the northern states.
He got cold feet at the last moment and withdrew from the business venture.
The celebration got out of hand when uninvited guests started arriving.
The police got wind of the smuggling operation and raided the warehouse.

meaning_en: Ability to speak fluently and persuasively
example: A successful salesperson must have the gift of the gab and great patience.
Any successful partnership requires genuine give and take from both sides.
After the argument, he gave his colleague the cold shoulder for a week.

meaning_en: To be contrary to one's nature
example: Accepting bribes goes completely against the grain of his personal values.
Despite several interventions, the patient's condition went from bad to worse.
Good discipline and academic excellence truly go hand in hand.
The customer service team went the extra mile to resolve every complaint.
Ever since the founder left, the once-reputed institution has gone to the dogs.
This scholarship is a golden opportunity that you must not let slip away.
The government must grasp the nettle and take tough decisions on taxation.
Both scientists independently discovered the same solution — great minds think alike.
The legal jargon in the contract was entirely Greek to me without a lawyer.
Being a greenhorn in the industry, he made many avoidable mistakes at first.
Despite his age, the retired general is hale and hearty and fully active.
The corrupt official was working hand in glove with the contractors all along.
There are no hard and fast rules for creativity; experimentation is key.

meaning_en: Difficult to solve or deal with
example: Winning over this customer base is a hard nut to crack for the new brand.

word: Have a finger in every pie
meaning_en: To be involved in many activities
example: He has a finger in every pie — politics, business, media, and charity.
She has an axe to grind against the management and that colours all her feedback.

word: Have one's heart in one's mouth
example: My heart was in my mouth as I waited for the final interview result.

meaning_en: To be successful in the end
example: After years of being underestimated, she finally had the last laugh at the awards.
In terms of analytical skill, she is head and shoulders above her peers.
The volunteers worked heart and soul to ensure the event was a grand success.
He witnessed the suffering and did nothing — he must have a heart of stone.
Clearing the two-year backlog in just one month was a Herculean task for the team.
He left his business partner high and dry without any warning.

word: Hit the nail on the head
example: You have hit the nail on the head — inefficiency is the root cause here.
Even when insulted, she held her tongue and walked away calmly.
The accused's alibi simply does not hold water given the available evidence.

meaning_en: A situation with two difficult choices
example: Caught between loyalty and honesty, she was on the horns of a dilemma.
He blows hot and cold about the deal — no one knows his real intention.
In a nutshell, the project failed because of poor communication and no planning.
All the conditions were clearly stated in black and white in the agreement.
The assassination was carried out in cold blood with no apparent motive.
He found himself in deep water when the audit exposed his fraudulent records.
Ever since she helped the director, she has been in his good books.
The celebrity is in hot water after the controversial video surfaced online.
The outcome of the election is still very much in the air at this point.

word: In the blink of an eye
example: The entire accident happened in the blink of an eye before anyone could react.
Investing in quality education pays off enormously in the long run.
The firefighters arrived in the nick of time before the blaze spread further.
He condemned the protest and praised it in the same breath — very contradictory.
She completed the project in the teeth of fierce opposition from her colleagues.
Ethnic fusion wear is very much in vogue this wedding season.

word: Iron hand in a velvet glove
example: The general was known for ruling with an iron hand in a velvet glove.
She has too many irons in the fire and struggles to focus.
You cannot only blame the child — it takes two to tango, after all.

meaning_en: A person who can do many different kinds of work
example: He is a jack of all trades but master of none — tries everything, excels in little.
His behaviour is completely Jekyll and Hyde — charming in public, cruel at home.
The new research centre is the jewel in the university's crown.
He acts like he invented the concept, but he is really a Johnny-come-lately in this field.
The auditors checked the books and did not miss a jot or tittle.

word: Judge a book by its cover
example: The scruffy applicant turned out to be brilliant — never judge a book by its cover.
Once the trend went viral, every brand jumped on the bandwagon immediately.
The reporter jumped the gun and announced the winner before votes were counted.
After years of corruption, he finally got his just deserts in court.

meaning_en: To keep something away or under control
example: Regular exercise helps keep many lifestyle diseases at bay in the long run.
With two part-time jobs, he barely manages to keep body and soul together.
The host tried hard to keep everyone in good humour with jokes and games.
Keep your fingers crossed — the final decision will be announced tomorrow.
Despite the chaos around her, she kept her head and handled the crisis well.

The old tycoon kicked the bucket before he could see his empire rebuilt.

word: Kill two birds with one stone
meaning_en: To achieve two results with one effort
example: By visiting both clients in one trip, she killed two birds with one stone.
After the disaster, he was surrounded by his kith and kin for support.

word: Knock one's head against a brick wall
meaning_en: To waste effort on impossible task
example: Trying to convince him without data is like knocking your head against a brick wall.

word: Knock the wind out of someone
example: The sudden demotion completely knocked the wind out of the ambitious manager.
After three months in the role, she finally knows the ropes and works independently.
Missing an exam for a lame excuse like traffic is simply unacceptable.
His third mistake in a week was the last straw that led to his dismissal.
By making that claim without evidence, he became the laughing stock of the seminar.
The whistleblower laid bare years of systematic corruption within the department.
The search party left no stone unturned in looking for the missing child.
Could you lend me a hand with these heavy boxes, please?
After the heated argument, both sides agreed to let bygones be bygones.

word: Let the cat out of the bag
example: He let the cat out of the bag about the surprise farewell party.

word: Like a bolt from the blue
example: The sudden announcement of a merger came like a bolt from the blue.
Both are stubborn and refuse to listen — like father, like son, indeed.
Despite equal effort, one partner took the lion's share of the credit.
Many daily wage workers live from hand to mouth and have no savings.
Think carefully before resigning — always look before you leap.
Do not lose heart after one failure; keep trying persistently.
He lost his temper during the meeting and shouted at everyone.
Team morale was at a low ebb after the third consecutive defeat.

word: Make a mountain out of a molehill
example: A small delay is nothing — stop making a mountain out of a molehill.

meaning_en: To manage expenses with limited income
example: With a single salary and three children, they struggle to make both ends meet.

word: Make hay while the sun shines
meaning_en: To use opportunity at the right time
example: Invest now while markets are rising — make hay while the sun shines.
She made up her mind to quit the corporate job and pursue teaching.
Trust him completely — he is a man of his word.
Rabindranath Tagore was a great man of letters and a Nobel laureate.
The chess champion finally met his match in the young prodigy.
He needs to mend his ways before it is too late for reform.
His milk and water response failed to convince anyone in the room.
Mind one's own business is commonly used.
He missed the bus by not investing in real estate ten years ago.
The renovation work is more or less complete; only finishing remains.
Move heaven and earth is commonly used.
Much ado about nothing is commonly used.

word: Nail one's colours to the mast
example: She nailed her colours to the mast by publicly endorsing the reform.
He had a narrow escape when the car skidded just inches from the wall.
The disruptive student was thrown out neck and crop from the hall.
Cooperation and unity are truly the need of the hour in these times.
The new policy is neither fish nor fowl and satisfies no one.

There is clearly no love lost between the two rival politicians.
Despite the long explanation, he left none the wiser about the procedure.
Early morning jogging is simply not my cup of tea at all.
Since one party violated the terms, the contract is now null and void.
She was on cloud nine after receiving the promotion she had worked for.
He felt on edge all morning waiting for the results to be announced.
A strict teacher keeps students on their toes throughout the year.
The struggling firm is on the brink of bankruptcy without fresh investment.

word: On the spur of the moment
example: He decided on the spur of the moment to resign from the post.
She visits her hometown only once in a blue moon these days.
My heart sank when I heard that my closest friend had been transferred.

meaning_en: Known by many though supposed secret
example: Their affair is an open secret that everyone knows but pretends not to.
She has been out of sorts ever since she heard the bad news.
He called me out of the blue after ten years of no contact.
Filling out all these government forms is a real pain in the neck.
Long working hours are part and parcel of a career in medicine.
He always passes the buck to his junior whenever there is a problem.
He paid through the nose for the concert tickets at the last minute.

meaning_en: Careful in small but wasteful in big
example: Skipping regular car maintenance is penny wise and pound foolish in the end.
A talented person like her should not have to play second fiddle.
By ignoring the safety rules, the workers are playing with fire.
Relax — I was only pulling your leg about the surprise inspection.
When things got out of control, the manager put her foot down firmly.
When she saw the clues, she quickly put two and two together.
The new neighbour is a real queer fish who rarely speaks to anyone.
She is always quick on the draw with sharp answers in debates.

meaning_en: A deceptive calm before a crisis
example: The negotiations seemed peaceful, but insiders called it the quiet before the storm.
After he repaid the favour, they decided they were quits with each other.
The match was abandoned because it started to rain cats and dogs.
If you read between the lines of his letter, his unhappiness is clear.
Winning the national award was truly a red letter day in her career.
The project was delayed for months because of unnecessary government red tape.
A champion never rests on his laurels but keeps training every day.

Sharma is the director's right hand man for all financial decisions.
As a rule of thumb, spend no more than a third of income on rent.
After losing his job, he quickly ran into debt to pay his bills.
The accused had to run the gauntlet of media cameras outside the court.
After the earthquake, rescuers found the trapped miners safe and sound.

meaning_hi: झूठी पहचान के साथ काम करना
meaning_en: To pretend to be something one is not
example: He sailed under false colours, claiming expertise he clearly did not have.

meaning_en: To do something that prevents failure
example: The last-minute sponsorship saved the day and the event went ahead as planned.
The two partners rarely see eye to eye on financial matters.
The new smartphone model sold like hot cakes on the first day itself.
She set the ball rolling with an inspiring opening speech to the team.
When things got difficult, he finally showed his true colours.
A good leader cannot afford to sit on the fence on important issues.

meaning_en: To be very revealing without words
example: The way the audience fell silent spoke volumes about the quality of her performance.
Someone in the team spilled the beans about the surprise party.

word: Strike while the iron is hot
example: The company should strike while the iron is hot and expand now.
The principal took the students to task for disrupting the examination.

word: Take with a pinch of salt
example: You should take his tall claims with a generous pinch of salt.

meaning_en: The final option when all others have failed
example: Surgery should be considered only as a last resort when all other treatments fail.
True friends stand by you through thick and thin without judgment.
His senior threw cold water on all his innovative project proposals.
New recruits are expected to toe the line without question at first.

meaning_en: Said humorously and not meant seriously
example: His remark about the government's efficiency was entirely tongue in cheek.
The authorities turned a blind eye to illegal construction in the area.
The officials turned a deaf ear to the farmers' repeated complaints about the drought.
After his release, he genuinely turned over a new leaf and changed.
In the second half, the team turned the tables and won convincingly.
They had to twist his arm before he finally agreed to testify.
He resigned under a cloud after the audit revealed financial irregularities.
The fraud was happening right under the manager's nose for two years.
She called in sick, saying she was feeling a bit under the weather.

meaning_en: Kept secret or not yet revealed
example: The new product launch plans are being kept under wraps until next month.
Residents were up in arms over the sudden closure of the local hospital.
His recent performance is simply not up to the mark expected of a senior official.
Every marriage has its ups and downs; patience is the key to survival.
The suspect vanished into thin air before police arrived at the location.
She managed the difficult team with an iron hand in a velvet glove.
Low supply and high demand made it a vendor's paradise in the housing market.
Unemployment and poverty form a vicious circle that is hard to break.

meaning_en: A person whose warnings or opinions are ignored
example: He was a voice in the wilderness when he warned about the financial risks years ago.

meaning_en: A sudden and complete reversal of opinion
example: After meeting the protesters, the minister did a volt face on the controversial policy.
She accepted him warts and all, which is the truest form of love.
He washed his hands of the entire project after the first dispute.
Their old argument is water under the bridge; they are friends again.

word: Wear one's heart on one's sleeve
example: She wears her heart on her sleeve and gets hurt very easily.
The family is well off and can afford the best education for their children.
He is still wet behind the ears and needs more time before leading a team.
The grand stadium built for the games became a white elephant afterwards.
The rumour spread like wildfire through the office before anyone could correct it.

meaning_en: A special quality that is hard to define
example: The young singer had that x factor that made the audience fall in love with her instantly.
On the treasure map, X marks the spot where the chest is buried.

meaning_en: Hostile or fearful attitude towards foreigners
example: His xenophobic attitude made integration with international colleagues very difficult.
His speech was a xerox copy of his previous one — nothing new at all.

meaning_en: Happening regularly every year without change
example: Year in, year out, she tops the examination without fail.
The tabloid was criticised for practising yellow journalism to boost sales.

word: You can't teach an old dog new tricks
meaning_en: It is hard to make people change old habits
example: He refuses to learn the new software — you simply cannot teach an old dog new tricks.
He treated his staff poorly and lost all support — you reap what you sow in the end.

word: You scratch my back and I scratch yours
example: Their deal was a classic case of you scratch my back and I scratch yours.
The troops were ready and waiting as zero hour finally approached.
The investigators quickly zeroed in on the prime suspect after reviewing the footage.

meaning_en: A strict policy of no exceptions to a rule
example: The school enforces zero tolerance towards bullying in any form.

meaning_en: An indirect or inconsistent path or approach
example: The politician took a zigzag course on the issue, confusing voters with contradictory statements.
He was told firmly to zip his lip and stop spreading rumours.
